result: The desk lamp hums like a tiny beehive. Beyond it the window is a flat black mirror, reflecting the page I’m writing on and the ghost of my own reflected eye, half‐open, half‐dreaming. I’ve come to believe that handwriting is the only honest surveillance system we have: a wormhole shaped by fear and hope alike, burrowing backward through the person you were eight minutes ago when you started the sentence. Ink is time’s fingerprint, even when time has nothing to say. Today the pen wants to talk about electrons; yesterday it was the smell of my grandfather’s tool shed. Thoughts arrive with the randomness of socks discovered behind the washing machine. Still, I line them up, grateful for their elastic warmth. The room is quiet except for the refrigerator’s lazy compressor, protesting the darkness with its small domestic earthquake. Somewhere in the building a neighbor coughs—a sound like tearing paper—and I feel an absurd solidarity with this stranger, the way red traffic lights blink in synchrony when no one is there to obey them. My watch says midnight, but the watch is a liar that counts only circles. Stretching, I realize I am both the museum and the visitor, shuffling through galleries of half-lit memories, clipping my own audio guide to my lapel. An ancient Greek philosopher believed the soul wandered at night when the eyelids closed their tiny curtains. If that’s true, then right now I’m probably somewhere down the street, pressing my hands against a stranger’s rain-wet bay window, surprised but not ashamed to see so much dust inside.

I’ve come to believe that handwriting is the only honest surveillance system we have: a wormhole shaped by fear and hope alike, burrowing backward through the person you were eight minutes ago when you started the sentence. Ink is time’s fingerprint, even when time has nothing to say. Today the pen wants to talk about electrons; yesterday it was the smell of my grandfather’s tool shed. Thoughts arrive with the randomness of socks discovered behind the washing machine. Still, I line them up, grateful for their elastic warmth. The room is quiet except for the refrigerator’s lazy compressor, protesting the darkness with its small domestic earthquake. Somewhere in the building a neighbor coughs—a sound like tearing paper—and I feel an absurd solidarity with this stranger, the way red traffic lights blink in synchrony when no one is there to obey them. My watch says midnight, but the watch is a liar that counts only circles. Stretching, I realize I am both the museum and the visitor, shuffling through galleries of half-lit memories, clipping my own audio guide to my lapel. An ancient Greek philosopher believed the soul wandered at night when the eyelids closed their tiny curtains. If that’s true, then right now I’m probably somewhere down the street, pressing my hands against a stranger’s rain-wet bay window, surprised but not ashamed to see so much dust inside.
